schools.summary: The four eastern kings: 'and it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of nations' — four kings of the east (Mesopotamia and Elam) named, the confederacy that warred against the cities of the plain (Shinar/Babylonia, Ellasar a city of Babylonia, Elam/Persia, the leading power under Chedorlaomer, and 'nations,' a king over various peoples, the historical setting of the war); these eastern kings (with Chedorlaomer of Elam at their head, vv.4-5) forming an alliance and coming against the kings of the plain (the campaign of the great eastern powers into Canaan, the earliest recorded war in Scripture, the setting for Lot's capture, v.12, and Abram's rescue); 'Tidal king of nations (Goiim)' (a king over various peoples or tribes, a ruler of mixed nations, the four-king confederacy of the east, led by Chedorlaomer, that subdued the cities of the plain and would carry off Lot) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

themes: And it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of nations
the four eastern kings who made war
the confederacy of the east, led by Chedorlaomer of Elam
the earliest recorded war in Scripture

schools.summary: The five kings of the plain: 'that these made war with Bera king of Sodom, and with Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, and Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela, which is Zoar' — the five kings of the cities of the plain (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela/Zoar, the local kings who had been subject to Chedorlaomer, v.4, and now suffered his war, the cities of the plain, soon to be destroyed, ch. 19); the five kings of the plain arrayed against the four eastern kings (the war of the four against the five, the cities of the plain, including Sodom, where Lot dwelt, 13:12, drawn into the conflict); 'Bela (the same is Zoar)' (Bela, also called Zoar, the little city, to which Lot would later flee, 19:22, the fifth of the cities of the plain, the identification of the city) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

themes: That these made war with Bera king of Sodom, and with Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, and Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela, which is Zoar
the five kings of the plain against whom the war was made
the cities of the plain, subject to Chedorlaomer, drawn into the conflict
Bela/Zoar, to which Lot would later flee (19:22)

schools.summary: The vale of Siddim: 'all these were joined together in the vale of Siddim, which is the salt sea' — the five kings of the plain joined together (allied, or gathered for battle) in the vale of Siddim (the valley which, the text notes, 'is the salt sea,' the Dead Sea, that is, the region later became, or was covered by, the salt sea, the place of the battle); the vale of Siddim a fertile valley where the cities of the plain lay (the kings gathering there for battle against the eastern confederacy, the valley of the conflict); 'which is the salt sea' (the narrator's note that the vale of Siddim is, or became, the salt sea, the Dead Sea, on one view, after the destruction of Sodom, ch. 19, the region submerged and became part of the Dead Sea, the note marking the later geography, the place of the battle, later covered by the salt sea) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: All these were joined together in the vale of Siddim, which is the salt sea
the joining of the kings in the vale of Siddim
the valley of the cities of the plain, the place of the battle
the region later covered by the salt sea (the Dead Sea)

schools.summary: The servitude and rebellion: 'twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled' — the kings of the plain having been subject (tributary) to Chedorlaomer of Elam for twelve years (in the thirteenth year they rebelling, refused tribute, threw off his yoke, the rebellion that provoked Chedorlaomer's punitive campaign, v.5, the cause of the war); the cities of the plain having been subject to the eastern king for twelve years (the dominion of Elam over the region, the servitude that the cities sought to cast off); 'in the thirteenth year they rebelled' (the cities revolting against Chedorlaomer, the rebellion bringing the eastern kings back to subdue them, the occasion of the campaign, vv.5-7, and of Lot's capture, v.12, the political cause of the war that would draw in Abram) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled
the cities' twelve years' servitude and their rebellion
the dominion of Elam and the revolt that provoked the war
the cause of the campaign that led to Lot's capture

schools.summary: Chedorlaomer's campaign: 'and in the fourteenth year came Chedorlaomer, and the kings that were with him, and smote the Rephaims in Ashteroth Karnaim, and the Zuzims in Ham, and the Emims in Shaveh Kiriathaim' — in the fourteenth year (after the rebellion, v.4), Chedorlaomer and his allied kings marching to subdue the rebels and the surrounding peoples (the punitive campaign of the eastern confederacy); the eastern kings smiting various peoples east and south of the Dead Sea — the Rephaim (giants, at Ashteroth Karnaim), the Zuzim (at Ham), the Emim (at Shaveh Kiriathaim), ancient and powerful peoples (some of gigantic stature, Deuteronomy 2:10-11, 20, the campaign subduing the region); the campaign of Chedorlaomer sweeping through the lands east of Jordan, smiting the Rephaim, Zuzim, and Emim (the eastern kings subduing the region before turning on the cities of the plain, v.8, the wide extent of the punitive war) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And in the fourteenth year came Chedorlaomer, and the kings that were with him, and smote the Rephaims... the Zuzims... and the Emims
Chedorlaomer's campaign against the peoples east of Jordan
the ancient peoples smitten (giants, Deuteronomy 2:10-11)
the wide sweep of the punitive war

schools.summary: The Horites of Seir smitten: 'and the Horites in their mount Seir, unto Elparan, which is by the wilderness' — the eastern kings also smiting the Horites (the cave-dwellers, the original inhabitants of mount Seir, later dispossessed by Esau's descendants, Deuteronomy 2:12, 22) in their mountain region of Seir (the campaign reaching as far as El-paran, by the wilderness, toward the south, near the desert, the southward extent of the war); the Horites of Seir — the inhabitants of the mountainous region south-east of the Dead Sea (smitten by Chedorlaomer's campaign, the peoples of Seir subdued); 'unto El-paran, which is by the wilderness' (the campaign reaching El-paran, perhaps near the gulf of Aqaba, or the edge of the wilderness of Paran, the southern limit of the war, the eastern kings sweeping down through the lands east and south of the Dead Sea, the wide reach of the punitive campaign) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the Horites in their mount Seir, unto Elparan, which is by the wilderness
the smiting of the Horites in mount Seir
the Horites of Seir subdued (Deuteronomy 2:12), the southward reach
the wide sweep of the punitive campaign

schools.summary: The return and smiting of the Amalekites and Amorites: 'and they returned, and came to Enmishpat, which is Kadesh, and smote all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites, that dwelt in Hazezontamar' — the eastern kings turning back (from the southern limit) and coming to En-mishpat (Kadesh), smiting the country of the Amalekites and the Amorites of Hazezon-tamar (En-gedi, the campaign sweeping back northward toward the cities of the plain); 'the country of the Amalekites' named by anticipation (the Amalekites, descendants of Esau's grandson Amalek, 36:12, not yet in being in Abram's day, the country named by the name it later bore, the region later held by the Amalekites, the campaign smiting that country); 'the Amorites... in Hazezon-tamar' (En-gedi, by the Dead Sea, the Amorites of that place smitten, the campaign drawing near the cities of the plain, Sodom, etc., the eastern kings, having subdued the surrounding peoples, now turning on the rebellious cities, v.8) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And they returned, and came to Enmishpat, which is Kadesh, and smote all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites, that dwelt in Hazezontamar
the eastern kings' return and their smiting of the Amalekites and Amorites
the country of the Amalekites named by anticipation (36:12)
the campaign nearing the cities of the plain

schools.summary: The five kings give battle: 'and there went out the king of Sodom, and the king of Gomorrah, and the king of Admah, and the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (the same is Zoar;) and they joined battle with them in the vale of Siddim' — the five kings of the plain going out and arraying themselves for battle against the four eastern kings in the vale of Siddim (v.3, the local kings, facing the punitive campaign, giving battle, the engagement of the four kings against the five); the five kings of the plain arrayed for battle (the cities of the plain making their stand against Chedorlaomer's confederacy, the desperate defense against the eastern power); 'they joined battle... in the vale of Siddim' (the battle joined in the valley, later the salt sea, v.3, the five kings against the four, the engagement that the plain-kings would lose, v.10, leading to the sack of Sodom and Lot's capture, vv.11-12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And there went out the king of Sodom... and they joined battle with them in the vale of Siddim
the five kings of the plain going out to battle
the cities of the plain making their stand against the eastern confederacy
the engagement of four kings against five, the battle that would be lost

schools.summary: Four kings with five: 'with Chedorlaomer the king of Elam, and with Tidal king of nations, and Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar; four kings with five' — the four eastern kings (named again, with Chedorlaomer of Elam at their head) arrayed against the five kings of the plain ('four kings with, against, five,' the disproportion noted, yet the four prevailed, the engagement of the two confederacies); Chedorlaomer of Elam leading the eastern confederacy (the chief of the four kings, to whom the cities had been subject, v.4, the leader of the campaign); 'four kings with five' (the four eastern kings, fewer in number, facing the five kings of the plain, yet the four, the seasoned eastern power, defeating the five, v.10, the note marking the odds, the victory of the four, who carried off the spoil and Lot, vv.11-12, the setting for Abram's rescue with a mere three hundred eighteen men, v.14, a greater disproportion still) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: With Chedorlaomer the king of Elam, and with Tidal king of nations, and Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar; four kings with five
the four eastern kings arrayed against the five
Chedorlaomer of Elam, the leader of the confederacy
the disproportion of forces, yet the four prevailed

schools.summary: The defeat of the plain-kings: 'and the vale of Siddim was full of slimepits; and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and fell there; and they that remained fled to the mountain' — the valley full of pits of bitumen (asphalt, plentiful in that region, the pits becoming a snare in the battle and flight, the natural hazard of the valley that turned the rout into disaster); 'the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and fell there' (the kings of the plain, defeated, fleeing and falling into the slimepits, or falling/perishing there, the rout of the plain-kings, their flight into the very pits of the valley, the disaster of the defeat, the king of Sodom, it seems, escaping, since he appears later, v.17); 'they that remained fled to the mountain' (the survivors fleeing to the hills for refuge, the scattering of the defeated, the rout complete, the cities open to plunder, v.11, and Lot taken, v.12) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the vale of Siddim was full of slimepits; and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and fell there; and they that remained fled to the mountain
the defeat and flight of the kings of the plain
the bitumen pits a snare in the rout
the cities left open to plunder and Lot's capture

schools.summary: The plundering of Sodom: 'and they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their victuals, and went their way' — the victorious eastern kings plundering the defeated cities, taking all their goods and provisions (victuals), and departing (the sack of Sodom and Gomorrah, the spoil carried off by the conquerors, the cities stripped); the eastern kings taking the goods and food of the cities (the complete plundering of the defeated, the wealth and provisions of the plain carried away); 'went their way' (the conquerors, laden with spoil, and with captives, including Lot, v.12, departing northward toward their own lands, the retreat of the victorious kings, the setting for Abram's pursuit, v.14, the plunderers carrying off the goods of Sodom, and Lot among the captives) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their victuals, and went their way
the plundering of Sodom and Gomorrah
the complete sack of the defeated cities
the conquerors departing with the spoil, and Lot among the captives

schools.summary: The capture of Lot: 'and they took Lot, Abram's brother's son, who dwelt in Sodom, and his goods, and departed' — among the captives and spoil, the eastern kings taking Lot (Abram's nephew) and his goods (Lot, caught up in the disaster of Sodom, the capture that would draw Abram into the war); the telling phrase — 'who dwelt in Sodom' (Lot, who had at first pitched his tent toward Sodom, 13:12, now dwelling in the city itself, his gradual drawing into Sodom, and now sharing in Sodom's calamity, taken captive with the city, the consequence of his worldly choice, 13:10-11, he who chose the plain for its richness now suffering with the wicked city, the danger of dwelling among the ungodly); 'and his goods, and departed' (Lot losing his goods, the very wealth for which he had chosen Sodom's plain, and his liberty, the worldly gain turned to loss, the captive carried off, the providence that would bring Abram to his rescue, v.14, and would, in time, draw Lot out of Sodom before its destruction, ch. 19) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And they took Lot, Abram's brother's son, who dwelt in Sodom, and his goods, and departed
the capture of Lot, who dwelt in Sodom
Lot's declension from pitching toward Sodom (13:12) to dwelling in it
the fruit of his worldly choice, his gain turned to loss

schools.summary: Abram the Hebrew and his confederates: 'and there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew; for he dwelt in the plain of Mamre the Amorite, brother of Eshcol, and brother of Aner: and these were confederate with Abram' — a fugitive from the battle bringing word to Abram of Lot's capture (Abram here first called 'the Hebrew,' ha-ibri, perhaps from Eber, 10:21, 24, or 'the one from beyond [the river],' the report that moved Abram to act); Abram called 'the Hebrew' (the descendant of Eber, or the man 'from beyond the Euphrates,' the title marking him as a distinct people, a stranger and sojourner among the Canaanites, the name by which the chosen line would be known); Abram dwelling at Mamre (13:18), and Mamre, Eshcol, and Aner (Amorite chiefs) 'confederate with Abram,' allied with him (the patriarch having made a league with these local Amorites, they joining him in the rescue, v.24, the alliances of Abram in the land, and the help they gave) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew... and these were confederate with Abram
the report brought to Abram the Hebrew and his confederates
Abram 'the Hebrew' (from Eber, or 'from beyond the river')
Mamre, Eshcol, and Aner, the Amorite chiefs allied with Abram

schools.summary: Abram arms his servants: 'and when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive, he armed his trained servants, born in his own house, three hundred and eighteen, and pursued them unto Dan' — Abram, hearing of Lot's capture, resolving to rescue him (the patriarch's love and loyalty to his kinsman, despite Lot's earlier choice, ch. 13, he acting promptly to deliver Lot); Abram 'armed his trained servants (his trained men, his retainers), born in his own house, three hundred and eighteen' (Abram's own household servants, born and bred in his house, trained and trusty, numbering three hundred eighteen, the considerable household of the patriarch, a sign of his greatness, the small band with which he would pursue the victorious kings, the disproportion, 318 men against the armies of four kings, marking the faith and courage of Abram, and the help of God); 'pursued them unto Dan' (Abram pursuing the retreating kings as far as Dan, in the north of Canaan, the place named by anticipation, by the name it later bore, Judges 18:29, the swift pursuit of the patriarch, the faith and resolve that led him to attack a far greater force) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive, he armed his trained servants, born in his own house, three hundred and eighteen, and pursued them unto Dan
Abram arming his servants to rescue Lot
the 318 trained household servants, a small band against four kings
the faith, courage, and loyalty of the patriarch

schools.summary: Abram's night attack: 'and he divided himself against them, he and his servants, by night, and smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus' — Abram dividing his small force (a military stratagem, attacking from several sides) and falling upon the enemy by night (the surprise night attack, the patriarch's wisdom and courage in the assault, the small band, by stratagem and God's help, routing the victorious kings); Abram 'smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand (north) of Damascus' (the night attack routing the enemy, and Abram pursuing them far to the north, beyond Damascus, the victory of the patriarch over a far greater force, the rout of the four kings, the deliverance accomplished); Abram's victory over the four kings (with their armies) by a band of three hundred eighteen the work of God (the faith of the patriarch, and the divine help that gave him the victory, the LORD delivering the enemies into his hand, v.20, the deliverance of Lot accomplished by God's power through Abram's faith and courage) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And he divided himself against them, he and his servants, by night, and smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus
Abram's night attack and his smiting of the kings
the surprise stratagem and the rout pursued beyond Damascus
the victory the work of God through Abram's faith

schools.summary: Abram recovers Lot and the spoil: 'and he brought back all the goods, and also brought again his brother Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the people' — Abram recovering all that the kings had taken: the goods of Sodom, his nephew Lot and his goods, and the women and people (the captives, the complete deliverance, the patriarch restoring all the spoil and freeing all the captives); Abram especially 'brought again his brother Lot, and his goods' (the rescue of Lot, the object of the expedition, the nephew delivered from captivity, Abram's love and loyalty rewarded with success); 'the women also, and the people' (all the captives, women and men, freed, the complete deliverance of the people of Sodom and the spoil, the victory full and the recovery total, the patriarch, having routed the kings, restoring all that was lost) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And he brought back all the goods, and also brought again his brother Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the people
Abram's recovery of Lot, the captives, and the goods
the rescue of Lot, the object of the expedition
the complete recovery of the spoil and the captives

schools.summary: The king of Sodom meets Abram: 'and the king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer, and of the kings that were with him, at the valley of Shaveh, which is the king's dale' — the king of Sodom (who had survived, v.10) coming out to meet Abram, victorious and returning with the spoil (the meeting that would lead to the king's offer, v.21, and Abram's refusal, vv.22-23, the welcome of the victor); Abram returning 'from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer, and of the kings that were with him' (from the defeat of the four kings, the victorious patriarch, having routed the eastern confederacy, the conqueror returning); 'the valley of Shaveh, which is the king's dale' (a valley near Salem/Jerusalem, later called the king's dale, cf. 2 Samuel 18:18, the place of the meeting, where both the king of Sodom and Melchizedek, v.18, came to Abram, the setting for the contrast between the two kings — the king of Sodom and the king of Salem) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer... at the valley of Shaveh, which is the king's dale
the king of Sodom going out to meet the returning Abram
the victorious patriarch met in the king's dale near Salem
the setting for the meeting with Melchizedek and the contrast of the two kings

schools.summary: Melchizedek, type of Christ: 'and Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine: and he was the priest of the most high God' — the grammatical-historical and many holding he was a real, historical king-priest of Salem (Jerusalem), a worshipper of the true God (El Elyon) amid the Canaanites, who, as both king and priest, was a type of Christ (Hebrews 7), 'Melchizedek' meaning 'king of righteousness,' and 'king of Salem' meaning 'king of peace' (Hebrews 7:2), without recorded genealogy, beginning, or end of life in the narrative, prefiguring Christ's eternal, royal priesthood; a Jewish tradition (noted by Gill) identifying Melchizedek with Shem the son of Noah (still living), but this rejected by many, since Shem's parentage and pedigree are known (whereas Melchizedek is 'without father, without mother, without descent,' Hebrews 7:3); some holding he was Christ himself (a Christophany), but the dominant Christian view (with Hebrews 7) taking him as a real man, a type of Christ; Melchizedek 'the priest of the most high God (El Elyon),' a true priest of the true God, outside the line of Abraham and before the Levitical priesthood (his priesthood, royal and not by descent, the pattern of Christ's priesthood, 'after the order of Melchizedek,' Psalm 110:4; Hebrews 5-7, the king-priest who blessed Abram and received his tithe, a type of the royal Priest, Christ) — Gill, Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin.

Clarke and the dominant reading on the bread and wine: brought to refresh Abram and his weary men (a hospitable provision after the battle); Clarke rejecting as 'an idle conceit' the notion that Melchizedek offered them in the way of sacrifice; a second reading (the Roman Catholic) taking the bread and wine as a priestly offering or sacrifice, prefiguring the Eucharist, but the natural sense, and Hebrews (which dwells on Melchizedek's blessing and tithe, not on bread and wine as a sacrifice), supporting the refreshment-reading. The bread and wine divide between a refreshment for Abram's men and a sacrificial/Eucharistic offering.

debates.question: Who was Melchizedek, and what were the bread and wine?

sides.position: A real Canaanite king-priest of Salem, type of Christ (the dominant view, Hebrews 7); the bread and wine a refreshment for Abram's weary men
Shem the son of Noah (the Jewish tradition) — rejected by many since Shem's pedigree is known
Christ himself (a Christophany); or (Roman Catholic) the bread and wine a priestly sacrifice prefiguring the Eucharist

themes: And Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine: and he was the priest of the most high God
Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of the most high God (the great typological crux)
a real king-priest type of Christ (Hebrews 7) vs Shem vs Christ himself
the bread and wine a refreshment vs a sacrificial/Eucharistic offering

schools.summary: Melchizedek blesses Abram: 'and he blessed him, and said, Blessed be Abram of the most high God, possessor of heaven and earth' — Melchizedek, as priest of the most high God, blessing Abram (the priestly blessing, 'the less is blessed of the better,' Hebrews 7:7, so Melchizedek, the type of Christ, greater than Abram, blessing him, the king-priest pronouncing God's blessing on the patriarch); Melchizedek pronouncing 'Blessed be Abram of the most high God' (invoking God's blessing on Abram, the patriarch blessed in the name of the true God, the priestly benediction acknowledging Abram as blessed of God); 'the most high God, possessor (maker, owner) of heaven and earth' (Melchizedek naming God as El Elyon, the Most High, the possessor, creator and owner, of heaven and earth, the confession of the one true God, sovereign over all, the title that Abram would echo, v.22, the worship of the Most High amid the Canaanites, the true theology of Melchizedek the priest) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he blessed him, and said, Blessed be Abram of the most high God, possessor of heaven and earth
Melchizedek's blessing of Abram
the king-priest, greater than Abram, blessing him (Hebrews 7:7)
God named the Most High, possessor (maker) of heaven and earth

schools.summary: Melchizedek blesses God, Abram tithes: 'and blessed be the most high God, which hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand. And he gave him tithes of all' — Melchizedek blessing (praising) God for giving Abram the victory (he ascribing the deliverance not to Abram's prowess but to God, the priest's thanksgiving, acknowledging God as the author of the victory, the true confession that God delivered the enemies into Abram's hand); Melchizedek giving the glory of the victory to God (the praise of the Most High for the deliverance, the priest leading in thanksgiving, the acknowledgment that the victory was God's gift, not man's achievement); 'he gave him tithes of all' (Abram giving Melchizedek a tenth of all, the spoil, the patriarch, acknowledging Melchizedek's priesthood and superior dignity, paying him tithes, Hebrews 7:4-10, the tithe a recognition of Melchizedek as priest of God, and so a type of Christ, to whom Abram, and in him Levi, paid tithes, the act marking Melchizedek's greatness and the honor due to God's priest) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And blessed be the most high God, which hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand. And he gave him tithes of all
Melchizedek blessing God and Abram giving him tithes
the victory ascribed to God, not Abram's prowess
Abram's tithe honoring Melchizedek's priesthood (Hebrews 7:4-10)

schools.summary: The king of Sodom's offer: 'and the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons, and take the goods to thyself' — the king of Sodom asking Abram to return the persons (the souls, the people, the captives Abram had recovered) but to keep the goods (the spoil) for himself (the offer that, by the law of war, the recovered spoil belonged to the victor, the king's proposal); the king asking for his people (the captives) back (the persons being of more value to the king than the goods, the request that Abram restore the people and keep the spoil); 'take the goods to thyself' (the king offering Abram the goods, the spoil he had recovered, as his by right of conquest, the temptation to enrich himself with the goods of Sodom, the offer that Abram would refuse, vv.22-23, lest the wicked king should claim to have made him rich, the test of Abram's disinterestedness) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons, and take the goods to thyself
the king of Sodom's offer to Abram
the king asking for his people and offering Abram the spoil
the temptation and test of Abram's disinterestedness

schools.summary: Abram's oath to the LORD: 'and Abram said to the king of Sodom, I have lift up mine hand unto the LORD, the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth' — Abram declaring that he had sworn (lifting up the hand being the gesture of an oath) to the LORD (the patriarch having bound himself by oath, the solemn resolve, made before God, governing his refusal of the spoil, v.23); Abram swearing by 'the LORD (Jehovah), the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth' (echoing Melchizedek's title for God, v.19, Abram confessing the LORD as the Most High, maker and owner of all, the patriarch identifying the true God, Jehovah, with the Most High whom Melchizedek served, the confession of the one true God before the king of Sodom, Abram's God the possessor of all, who needed not the gifts of the king); Abram's oath the ground of his refusal (trusting God who possessed all things, the patriarch taking nothing from the king of Sodom, v.23, the faith and integrity of Abram, who looked to God, not man, for his enrichment) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Abram said to the king of Sodom, I have lift up mine hand unto the LORD, the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth
Abram's oath to the LORD in refusing the king's offer
Abram echoing Melchizedek's confession of the true God (v.19)
the patriarch looking to God, not man, for his enrichment

schools.summary: Abram refuses the spoil: 'that I will not take from a thread even to a shoelatchet, and that I will not take any thing that is thine, lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich' — Abram refusing to take anything of the king of Sodom's, not the smallest thing (from a thread to a shoe-lace, the complete, scrupulous refusal, the patriarch not enriching himself with the goods of Sodom in the least particular); Abram taking nothing that belonged to the king (his refusal total, the patriarch's noble disinterestedness, refusing the spoil he might have claimed by right, the faith that looked to God, not to the wealth of Sodom); 'lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich' (Abram refusing, lest the wicked king should boast that he had enriched the patriarch, Abram owing his wealth to God alone, whose promise, 12:2, and gift were his riches, not to the king of Sodom, the concern for God's honor and his own integrity, that none might say man, not God, had made him rich, the magnanimity and faith of Abram) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: That I will not take from a thread even to a shoelatchet, and that I will not take any thing that is thine, lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich
Abram's refusal of the smallest part of Sodom's spoil
the patriarch's total, disinterested refusal of the spoil
that he might owe his wealth to God alone, not to the king of Sodom

schools.summary: Abram's exception for his men and allies: 'save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men which went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre; let them take their portion' — Abram excepting only what his young men (servants) had already consumed (their necessary provisions during the expedition), and the portion due to his allies (the patriarch's refusal, v.23, extending to his own gain, but he not imposing his abstinence on others); Abram reserving 'the portion of the men which went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre' (his Amorite confederates, 13:18; 14:13, who had joined the expedition, taking their rightful share of the spoil, Abram, while refusing for himself, not depriving his allies of their due, the justice and fairness of the patriarch); 'let them take their portion' (Abram's abstinence his own act of faith and disinterestedness, not a law imposed on his allies, he leaving them free to take their share, the patriarch's equity, generous to others while self-denying for himself, the balance of his integrity and his fairness) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: Save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men which went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre; let them take their portion
Abram's exception for his men and his allies' portion
the Amorite confederates' rightful share of the spoil
Abram's equity - self-denying for himself, just to others
